title: Plastic waste per capita imports vs. exports
caption:
Plastic waste per capita imports vs. exports, 2024
Imports per person (tonnes; plotted on a logarithmic axis)

This scatter plot maps the relationship between plastic waste exports and imports per capita for various countries in 2024, with both axes plotted on a logarithmic scale ranging from 0.000001 to over 10 tonnes. Points are color-coded by income group, distinguishing low-income, lower-middle-income, upper-middle-income, and high-income countries. A diagonal line indicates where imports equal exports; points above this line represent net importers, while those below represent net exporters. High-income countries, shown in dark green, are distributed across the chart but cluster notably in the upper-right quadrant, including Austria, Canada, and Norway, which show high values for both metrics, often exceeding 1 tonne per capita for both imports and exports. Conversely, several high-income nations like Iceland and Barbados appear in the lower-right, indicating high exports but low imports. Lower-middle-income countries, marked in light purple, frequently occupy the lower-left section, with nations like Nepal, Burundi, and Cote d'Ivoire registering values below 0.1 tonnes for both variables. Specific outliers include the Bahamas and Malaysia, which sit high on the vertical axis with imports exceeding 1 tonne per person, while maintaining moderate export levels. Meanwhile, countries such as Afghanistan and DR Congo appear in the upper-left area, signifying higher imports relative to their very low export volumes. The distribution reveals a wide disparity in per capita plastic waste trade, spanning several orders of magnitude from less than 0.0001 tonnes to over 10 tonnes depending on the specific nation and metric.
